title: OpenAI & Meta Distinguished Engineer (IC9) On Working With Zuck, Carmack & Career Growth | Philip Su
guest_role: Distinguished Engineer (IC9) at Meta, later OpenAI; ex-Microsoft
key_takeaways: What distinguished Mark Zuckerberg was his constant devotion to growth; John Carmack was one of the most impressive engineers Su worked with because of his prolific engineering ability — he could drop into a codebase he knew nothing about and give excellent feedback.

Su reached IC9 by founding and scaling Facebook's London office from around a dozen engineers to 500+, which gave him the blast radius to have IC9 impact; IC9 expectations are defined by the blast radius you have and what you can guarantee the success of.

The highest-level promotions are not a checklist of quantitative points; they run on qualitative feedback, needing the judgment of someone at a higher level to explain what makes the person's work special.

Su switched between IC and EM six times; for generalists there is a lot of commonality between management and tech leadership, which let him switch roles based on business need and personal enjoyment.
He eventually requested a demotion from IC9 to IC7 because he wanted to do more technical work; it relieved the pressure of IC9 expectations and preserved role-switching flexibility.
Generalists are much more effective at smaller companies; once companies get larger, specialists have more potential since they can go as deep as they want.
To write well, you need to read examples of good writing and take inspiration from it to improve your own.
